title: Apps Marketed to US Troops Are Shipping Chinese and Russian Code
gist: A Purdue/West Point/FIU study of 220+ apps marketed to US military personnel found more than one in eight contained code from China, Russia, or other foreign nations—including Huawei's HMS Core and Russia's Yandex—raising fears that adversaries could exploit the unregulated ad-tech supply chain to track troop deployments and locations of sensitive sites.

insights.claim: More than one in eight of 220+ apps marketed to US military personnel contained software built by companies in China, Russia, or other foreign nations, and roughly 7 percent carried third-party code from a Pentagon-designated adversary nation.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: Quantifies a supply-chain exposure that could invite new federal restrictions on foreign code in military-facing apps.
insights.locator: A recent examination of hundreds of mobile apps

insights.claim: Twelve apps contained Huawei's HMS Core, which advertises the ability to map user locations, deliver ads, and store images and video, and several of those were built for state National Guard organizations.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: Location-mapping code from a US-flagged security threat inside National Guard apps is a concrete espionage vector.
insights.locator: Twelve of the apps contained HMS Core

insights.claim: One popular app for rating base living conditions included Huawei code, while two others built by Russian companies incorporated the Russian ad service Yandex.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: Named adversary ad-tech embedded in troop-facing apps shows the threat is specific, not theoretical.
insights.locator: According to researchers at Purdue University

insights.claim: The researchers observed no data actually flowing to Huawei servers, but warn an SDK can be updated remotely at any time so dormant code today can become spyware tomorrow, and in one case Huawei code arrived without the developer's knowledge as a dependency in a commercial notification tool.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: Remote-updatable dependencies make the risk latent and hard to audit, undermining static app-vetting approaches.
insights.quote: Code that is dormant today can still be spyware tomorrow.
insights.locator: The researchers observed no data actually going

insights.claim: Nearly two-thirds (64 percent) of the military-marketed apps contained third-party SDKs used for analytics and advertising that can track user behavior and locations and share it with outside companies, with 76 distinct SDKs found and the most common from Google and Facebook.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: The pervasiveness of tracking SDKs shows the exposure is systemic to the ad-tech economy, not a few bad apps.

insights.claim: Forty percent of the apps collected or shared more data than they disclosed in their Google or Apple store listings.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: Widespread disclosure gaps undermine app-store privacy labels as a trust mechanism, a target for audit regulation.
insights.locator: Forty percent of the apps collected or shared more data

insights.claim: Neither Google's Play Store Data Safety section nor Apple's App Store Privacy Labels disclose the country of origin of software running inside an app, leaving users no straightforward way to know which apps carry adversary code.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: The missing country-of-origin disclosure is a concrete gap that policy or platform changes could address.
insights.locator: But no user has a straightforward way to know

insights.claim: In April, US Central Command confirmed to Senator Ron Wyden it had received multiple threat reports of adversaries exploiting commercial location data to target or surveil US personnel in the Middle East, the first official confirmation troops in an active war zone were being hunted through the data-broker economy.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: Official confirmation elevates data-broker exposure from privacy concern to active national-security threat, strengthening the case for legislation.
insights.locator: In April, US Central Command acknowledged

insights.claim: Location data harvested from ordinary apps has traced US service members to their homes, children's schools, and sensitive sites including intelligence facilities and hardened shelters where nuclear weapons are believed stored.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: The demonstrated ability to map sensitive-site routines makes the ad-tech data economy a strategic vulnerability.

insights.claim: In a survey of 103 military-affiliated Americans, more than 83 percent used at least one app with data practices that made them uncomfortable, averaging more than three such apps each, yet nearly two-thirds reported little or no institutional guidance on personal app use.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: The awareness-guidance gap points to demand for institutional and product-level mitigation solutions.

insights.claim: Participants ranked in-phone warnings about foreign or unknown third-party code as both the most effective and most-supported mitigation, with a data-broker restriction law, independent audits, and foreign-code bans drawing nearly identical support.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: Strong support for warnings and audits signals the likely shape of future regulation and product features in this space.
